title: Quiz: a query slowed down as the table grew
prompt_md: A query that was instant on a few thousand rows now takes seconds after the table grew to millions of rows. What is your first step to investigate?
options: Immediately add more RAM and CPU to the database server
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query to see the execution plan, look for a full table (sequential) scan or N+1 pattern, and add the missing index
Switch the database from PostgreSQL to a NoSQL store that scales better
Add a cache in front of the query so it only runs once
explanation_md: Always **measure before you change**. `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows the **query execution plan** and real timings, so you can spot a **sequential (full table) scan** where an **index seek** should be — the classic cause of a query that degrades as rows grow. Adding the missing **index** (on the column(s) in the `WHERE`/`JOIN`) turns an O(n) scan into a fast B-tree lookup. Also watch for the **N+1 query** pattern hiding in application code. Throwing hardware, NoSQL, or caching at it without reading the plan is guessing — and caching just hides a fixable indexing problem.
